project.name: dotfiles
project.description: Opinionated Bash/Zsh dotfiles for reproducible terminal environments across WSL, Linux, and macOS. A modular shell-config system: shared POSIX code in shell-common/ is sourced by both bash/ and zsh/ loaders; git/ manages a 2-tier hook system; tests/ holds bats + pytest suites.

nodes.name: plugin-sync-session.sh
nodes.summary: Claude Code SessionStart/Stop hook that snapshots the plugin SSOT files at session start and re-runs plugin-sync on Stop when they change, closing the sync gap left by UI-driven plugin commands (#1082).
nodes.languageNotes: Best-effort hook that always exits 0; watches ground-truth SSOT files to stay entry-path agnostic.

nodes.name: _snapshot
nodes.summary: Captures a baseline snapshot (content hashes and key sets) of the shared plugin manifest files for later drift comparison.

nodes.name: plugin-sync.sh
nodes.summary: Claude Code PostToolUse hook for `claude plugin ...` commands that merges public and private plugin manifests into the dotfiles SSOT so a fresh PC can rebuild its plugin set.
nodes.languageNotes: Always exits 0; bails when stdin is a terminal to avoid blocking on manual invocation.

nodes.name: _extract_target
nodes.summary: Parses the plugin-command event JSON to determine the target marketplace or plugin being mutated.

nodes.name: _commit_if_changed
nodes.summary: Commits the updated plugin manifest files to git when their content has changed, keeping the dotfiles SSOT in sync.

nodes.name: post-gh-pr-create.sh
nodes.summary: Claude Code PostToolUse hook for `gh pr create` that parses the new PR URL and syncs the GitHub project board (PR to In review, linked issues to In progress).
nodes.languageNotes: Single-owner sync design: the gh-pr skill auto-skips inline sync when this hook is installed.

nodes.name: _post_gh_pr_create_repo_has_board
nodes.summary: Checks whether the current repository has an associated GitHub project board before attempting board synchronization.

nodes.name: session-start-pc-context.sh
nodes.summary: Claude Code SessionStart hook that injects the current PC's setup-mode and hostname as additionalContext so per-machine facts reach each fresh session (#1052).
nodes.languageNotes: Emits no context and never blocks when the mode file is missing or unrecognized.

nodes.name: session-start-plugin-path-normalize.sh
nodes.summary: Claude Code SessionStart hook that rewrites plugin path fields in the shared plugin SSOT to the active $CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR spelling, fixing multi-account installLocation validation failures (#1098).
nodes.languageNotes: Works around Claude Code's literal string-prefix installLocation check that ignores symlink resolution.

nodes.name: _normalize_file
nodes.summary: Rewrites the plugin path fields inside a single shared manifest file to the active config-dir spelling.

nodes.name: session-start-settings-drift.sh
nodes.summary: Claude Code SessionStart hook that warns when the live settings.json `.hooks` block has drifted from the dotfiles SSOT, surfacing the silent failure where new hooks never fire until setup.sh is re-run (#1086).
nodes.languageNotes: Compares only the `.hooks` field since the Bedrock overlay touches env/model but never hooks.

nodes.name: skill_step_catalog.yml
nodes.summary: SSOT catalog for skill_completion_guard.py listing which multi-step skills have step-skip protection and the step IDs they must emit before a turn may end (#753).

nodes.name: marketplaces.json
nodes.summary: Public Claude Code plugin marketplace manifest mapping marketplace names to their GitHub source repositories, used as the SSOT for restore and reconcile operations.

nodes.name: plugins.json
nodes.summary: Public Claude Code plugin manifest listing installed plugins qualified by their marketplace, serving as the install SSOT for restore/reconcile.

nodes.name: publish-sync.sh
nodes.summary: Publishes locally-committed plugin manifest changes that cannot be pushed directly because both repos require PR-based merges; builds the publish commit with git plumbing against origin/main and opens/merges a PR.
nodes.languageNotes: Uses git plumbing (hash-object, read-tree into a scratch index, write-tree, commit-tree) to build commits without touching HEAD or the working index.

nodes.name: reconcile.sh
nodes.summary: Full-recompute drift detector and repair tool that rebuilds the dotfiles plugin manifests to exactly match the ~/.claude-shared SSOT, adding missing entries and pruning ghost entries the incremental hook can never remove.

nodes.name: restore.sh
nodes.summary: Reinstalls Claude Code plugins and marketplaces from the dotfiles manifest, resolving the correct account-routed CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR; add-only by default with an optional --sync flag to also prune surplus local items.

nodes.name: _repo_target
nodes.summary: Resolves the target repository (public dotfiles vs internal company nested repo) for a publish operation.

nodes.name: _fetch_origin
nodes.summary: Fetches origin/main to establish the base against which the publish commit is built.

nodes.name: _build_publish_commit
nodes.summary: Builds the manifest-sync commit entirely with git plumbing against origin/main, without checking out a branch or moving HEAD.

nodes.name: _publish_branch
nodes.summary: Pushes the built publish commit to a new remote branch to stage it for a pull request.

nodes.name: _open_and_merge_pr
nodes.summary: Opens a pull request for the publish branch and merges it, satisfying the branch-protection requirement that changes land via PR.

nodes.name: _publish_manifest_diff
nodes.summary: Orchestrates publishing a single repo's manifest diff: builds the commit, pushes the branch, and opens/merges the PR.

nodes.name: _cleanup_local_main_if_pure_sync
nodes.summary: Advances local main to the published origin/main (fast-forward or rebase) but only when every ahead commit is a pure manifest-sync commit and the working tree is clean, relying on patch-id to drop the redundant published commit.

nodes.name: _public_publish_allowed
nodes.summary: Guards whether publishing to the public repo is permitted in the current PC/setup mode.

nodes.name: _read_json_or
nodes.summary: Reads a JSON file returning a fallback default when the file is missing or unreadable.

nodes.name: _diff_marketplaces
nodes.summary: Computes the difference between the SSOT and the dotfiles marketplaces manifest to detect drift.

nodes.name: _diff_plugins
nodes.summary: Computes the difference between the SSOT and the dotfiles plugins manifest to detect drift.

nodes.name: _run_check
nodes.summary: Implements the default --check mode: prints the SSOT-vs-manifest diff and exits non-zero when drift is present.

nodes.name: _commit_if_changed
nodes.summary: Commits the reconciled manifest changes only when the files were actually modified.

nodes.name: _run_apply
nodes.summary: Implements --apply mode: rewrites the manifest to match the SSOT exactly and commits if changed.

nodes.name: _target_config_dirs
nodes.summary: Resolves the account-routed CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR target directories so plugin installs land in the config dir actually in use.

nodes.name: _restore_from
nodes.summary: Installs the marketplaces and plugins declared in the manifest into a given config directory.

nodes.name: _local_plugins
nodes.summary: Enumerates the plugins currently installed locally for comparison against the manifest.

nodes.name: _prune_pass
nodes.summary: Under --sync, prunes local marketplaces and plugins that are absent from the manifest so the local set matches the SSOT exactly.

nodes.name: _run_for_config_dir
nodes.summary: Runs the full restore (and optional prune) pass against a single resolved config directory.

tour.title: Project Overview
tour.description: Start with the README to understand what this project is: an opinionated, modular Bash/Zsh dotfiles system for reproducible terminal environments across WSL, Linux, and macOS. It lays out the three-layer split (shell-common, bash, zsh) and the quick-start flow, giving you the map for everything that follows. CLAUDE.md complements it with the architectural rules and critical conventions the codebase enforces.

tour.title: Installation & Symlinks
tour.description: Installation begins here. setup.sh is the top-level entry point that asks for your environment, configures .local.sh files, and wires shell configuration into place; install.sh creates the actual dotfiles symlinks (~/.bashrc, ~/.zshrc, ~/.gitconfig) by delegating to a create_symlink helper. shell-common/setup.sh is the shared bootstrapper that backs up existing files and safely symlinks or copies config. Understanding this step explains how the repo takes over your home directory without destroying what was there.
tour.languageLesson: Dotfiles are installed via symlinks rather than copies so the tracked repo remains the single source of truth: editing a file in the repo instantly updates the live config, and setup is idempotent because existing targets are backed up before relinking.

tour.title: How a Shell Boots
tour.description: Once symlinks exist, ~/.bashrc and ~/.zshrc point at these per-shell entry points. main.bash and main.zsh are the runtime bootstrappers: each sets up shell-specific behavior and then hands off to the shared loader so bash and zsh converge on the same portable core. Comparing the two shows how the project keeps shell-specific glue thin and pushes everything reusable into shell-common.
tour.languageLesson: Cross-shell code must avoid bash-only constructs: use POSIX '[ ]' tests and '>/dev/null 2>&1' redirection instead of '[[ ]]' and '&>', so the same file can be sourced safely by both bash and zsh.

tour.title: The Module Auto-Loader
tour.description: This is the heart of the boot sequence. loader.sh auto-sources the shell-common categories (aliases, env, functions) in order, so adding a new file to one of those directories makes it live on the next shell start with no manual wiring. shell-common/AGENTS.md documents the directory-placement decision tree that governs where new code belongs and the key rule that tools/custom/ is never auto-sourced. Every step after this one lives in a directory this loader walks.

tour.title: Environment Variables
tour.description: The first category the loader sources is env/. These files export environment variables grouped by purpose: development.sh sets general dev toggles shared across projects, while path.sh builds and normalizes PATH, deduplicating entries via a clean_paths helper. Splitting env by concern keeps machine-specific and tool-specific configuration isolated and easy to reason about.

tour.title: Aliases
tour.description: Next the loader sources aliases/, again split by purpose. core.sh holds everyday shortcuts including the 'src' reloader that re-sources your shell config after edits, and git.sh adds git-workflow aliases plus a hook_check helper that reports the status of the repo's git hooks. These files show the project's convention of small, single-topic modules rather than one monolithic aliases file.

tour.title: The Help System
tour.description: The functions/ category is the largest, and my_help.sh is its centerpiece: a centralized help registry and dispatcher that every dotfiles command registers into, organized by category. This is why a newcomer can discover functionality by topic instead of grepping source. Understanding the registry pattern here unlocks how the dozens of other functions expose themselves to users.

tour.title: The UX Presentation Library
tour.description: All user-facing output flows through ux_lib.sh, the central presentation library that provides colored, structured terminal primitives (ux_header, ux_success, ux_error, ux_info). The project forbids raw echo/printf/tput in app scripts so output stays consistent everywhere. UX_GUIDELINES.md codifies how and when to use each primitive, making this the shared vocabulary for every script's output.

tour.title: Tool Integrations
tour.description: Under tools/, integration files wire external CLIs into the shell on demand. nvm.sh is a representative example, providing a Node Version Manager install helper that only activates when relevant. This is the 'Adding a New Tool Integration' pattern from shell-common's docs: a self-contained integration file that plugs into the help and loader system rather than scattering setup across the codebase.

tour.title: Git Hooks
tour.description: Beyond the shell, the repo manages a 2-tier git hook system. hook-config.sh is the single source of truth for hook configuration, pre-push enforces protected-branch checks plus an upstream-leak guard, and pre-push-rules.sh holds the leak-guard patterns that stay inert until you export the activation variables. git/AGENTS.md explains the tiered design and escape hatches. Together they show how the project defends against accidental pushes and secret leaks.

tour.title: Claude / AI Integration
tour.description: The claude/ tree integrates Claude Code and AI tooling into the environment. claude/setup.sh copies settings and links skills/docs into each account's config directory, with settings.json copied as a real file (not symlinked) so '/model' writes never dirty the tracked repo, and statusline-command.sh renders the custom status line. claude/AGENTS.md documents the symlink-vs-copy scheme and the personal-override merge behavior. This is the newest and most opinionated subsystem of the dotfiles.

tour.title: Project Config & Linting
tour.description: Two config files define the developer toolchain that keeps the repo healthy. mise.toml is the command SSOT, declaring the lint/fix/test tasks referenced throughout CLAUDE.md, while pyproject.toml configures the Python tooling (ruff, mypy) for the integration tests. Knowing these explains how 'mise run lint' and 'mise run test' resolve to concrete tool invocations.
tour.languageLesson: mise.toml centralizes tools, environment, and tasks in one declarative file so contributors run the same commands regardless of machine — the tasks table acts as the project's command dictionary instead of a loose collection of shell scripts.

tour.title: The Test Suites
tour.description: Quality is enforced by a multi-runner test setup. tests/test is the single entry point that runs bats shell tests, pytest integration tests, and golden-rule checks together. test_help_topics.py exercises the help system from Step 7 end-to-end, and test_helper.bash provides the shared bats harness for the shell function tests. This is where the conventions taught in earlier steps get verified automatically.

tour.title: CI/CD Pipelines
tour.description: The capstone: GitHub Actions run the same checks on every push and pull request. ci.yml is the primary workflow orchestrating lint and test jobs with concurrency cancellation for superseded runs, and test.yml is the focused workflow that executes the repository test suite from Step 13. Seeing these ties the whole tour together — the local 'mise run lint/test' commands are exactly what CI enforces before code merges.
tour.languageLesson: GitHub Actions workflows use 'on' triggers to fire on push/PR, 'jobs' for parallel work, and concurrency groups to cancel superseded runs so only the latest commit's checks consume CI minutes.
